# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Dark discomfort beautiful movie requests please

**Score:** 43
**Body:** We want dark, we want discomfort, we want it to be nice to look at:  
(So help me God if its poorly done or isn’t visually pleasing you will be hearing from me)

Along the lines of:  
\- Nocturnal animals  
\- The road  
\- Mother  
\- The lighthouse  
\- A cure for wellness  
\- The House that Jack Built  
AND TOTALLY FORGOT (thanks for the reminder :D )  
\- Midsommar  
\- Hereditary 

Let’s go sickos, give me your best shots:

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pan’s Labyrinth 
2. “Melancholia” is my fav. 
3. Brazil
4. The Orphanage 
5. Annihilation is absolutely gorgeous and superb cosmic horror. 

Hellraiser 2 is surprisingly beautiful.
6. Melancholia 

City of Lost Children

Under the Skin

Mad God
7. Annihilation 
8. Antichrist (2009).  

Pretty challenging movie.  It’s a gorgeous movie, but a slow burn.  It ends with some of the most insane things I’ve seen on film.
9. Magnolia
10. Constantine 

## 2. Looking for erotic thrillers

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Need suggestions for an erotic thriller for the weekend. 

I've already watched Basic Instinct 1 and 2, Unfaithful, Obsession, Wild Things, Eyes Wide Shut, Fifty Shades of Grey series,. 

Prefer something similar to Basic Instinct or Fifty Shades of Grey with long intense scenes. 

But I don't want to watch porn as I'm looking for a good plot and intense (sex) action. 

Prefer English language. 

Note: It should be more graphic than thematic. Don't want Caligula like suggestions as it felt disturbing. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Body Heat (1981)  the one that basically defined the genre, steamy neo-noir with Kathleen Turner, great plot twists and genuinely intense. Also Disclosure (1994) with Michael Douglas and Demi Moore, and if you want something more recent try Deep Water (2022)  Ben Affleck and Ana de Armas, slow burn psychological thriller with a dark ending.
2. Bound (1996)
3. The Handmaiden (2016)
  - The Handmaiden is incredible but it's Korean with subtitles - OP mentioned English preference. Still worth watching though.
4. Chloe (2009)

Surprised no one's mentioned it yet
5. Lust Caution

Crash

Lost Highway

Disclosure

Jade

Fatal Attraction

Cruel Intentions

Sliver

Dressed to Kill

Belle de Jour

Eyes of Laura Mars

Sea of Love

The 4th Man

Decision to Leave

Body of Evidence 

Presume Innocent 

A Perfect Murder 

The Piano Teacher

The Handmaid

Body of Evidence

The Voyeurs

The Housemaid

Cruising

Bound

Body Double

Body Heat

The Crush

Fear

Femme

Live Flesh

Love Lies Bleeding

Single White Female

Sleeping with the Enemy

Stranger by the Lake

Swimming Pool

Sex Lies and Videotape

Femme Fatale

Exotica

Elle

American Gigolo

Babygirl

New Rose Hotel

The Last Seduction

To Die For

Dead Ringers

and lastly, Fatal Instinct as a parody of the genre.
6. Body Double ... 1984

Brian De Plama.

Sexy, sleepy, murder.....


7. if you want that basic instinct verhoeven energy then jade (1995) is right there, joe eszterhas wrote both, twisty erotic mystery thats pure 90s. and the last seduction (1994) is a must, linda fiorentino plays one of the best femme fatales ever, super sharp neo noir with a lot of tension and a great plot

last seduction is the one id push hardest imo, it leans more on the manipulation and dread than explicit stuff but its exactly that smart adult thriller lane youre describing
8. One of my top favourites is SLIVER  
man sharon stone is so hot in it I spend my childhood dreaming of her. 
9. Fatal Attraction (1987) is a must. 9  1/2 Weeks (1985) by the director of Unfaithful. The Last Seduction (1994). Bitter Moon (1992). Wild Orchid (1990). In The Realm Of The Senses (1976). Indecent Proposal (1993). The Postman Always Rings Twice (1981). Jade (1995) is a poor man's Basic Instinct but worth watching.

## 3. Movies where the people rise up

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I’m in that bad mental space of angry and disappointed at the state of things, and want some righteous bloody fury. 

Besides V for Vendetta (which I’ve seen too many times) what is out there that feels both relevant to today’s anxieties, and shows the people rising up to smash down oppression?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A Bug's Life
2. Pump Up the Volume, haha.
  - "Just look inside vourselt and you'll see me waving up at you naked wearing only a cock ring"
3. Independence Day
4. Sorry to Bother You 
5. Planet of the Apes 
6. Halan Co USA. For my money the best documentary ever made. 

Also

Newsies (not kidding it rules)

Amistad

Number 24

Norma Rae

The Great Escape
7. Can I recommend a 24 episode series? Or is that against the rules of the sub? 
8. Network (1976)
9. Fight Club. Maybe not bloody fury, but it’s about rising up.

## 4. Something non-anxiety for a night alone

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Hi! Will be alone for the coming few nights and need some kind of non-anxiety inducing distraction. It's been a while since I saw my last film (couple of months I think). So I am in for anything all-consuming that will distract my mind from an empty house. 

TIA for your troubles.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Sheep detectives
  - My new favorite movie, it’s so cute
2. Steel magnolias
3. Marcel The Shell With Shoes On
  - Oh it's that Jenny Slate movie! Added. Thanks. 
4. What do you like?
  - Often, slice of life. Everyday lives of people. It's peaceful, quiet and intriguing. Perfect Days is a good example. But a less pronounced version would do as well.
5. For when I'm not looking for anxiety, I tend to go to a couple Ghibli movies, namely Kiki's Delivery Service and My Neighbor Totoro haha. Good, easy-to-chew movies with minimal (or none at all) anxiety or conflict. I prefer Totoro because it feels almost more like a fantastical slice-of-life haha.
  - I just answered Slice of Life elsewhere. I have seen Kiki so maybe this is meant to be. I'll try and watch Totoro tonight.
6. Ted Lasso S1  is easy and fun with out the “shoe drop” moments. 

## 5. Lighthearted adventure movie set in the past (1900s), not made in the past to clarify

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I watched Christopher Robin and enjoyed it a lot. It’s lighthearted, has adventure, and set sometimes after 1945. Movies set in that era tend to be drama or action of wwII and it was refreshing to see something different from that time. I loved the atmosphere, the outfits and way of living and want to see something similar.   
Another movie that is kinda similar in description which people might suggest is the fantastic beasts but I found them boring and kinda gloomy. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh brother where art thou?

Raiders of the lost ark


2. The Mummy (1999)
3. The Secret Garden (1993)
4. Princess Bride 
5. I enjoyed this quite a lot “The Personal History of David Copperfield” (2019). Very colorful & fun but it’s set in the 1840s as it’s based on a Charles Dickens novel
6. Catch Me If You Can
7. **Stardust 2007**
8. The Mummy

The Rocketeer

High Road to China

The Shadow

The Sting

Hugo

The Man Who Would Be King

The Lost City of Z

Atlantis: The Lost Empire


9. Lion the Witch and the Wardrobe 
10. Who Framed Roger Rabbit?

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Cypher from The Matrix (1999) is the most duplicitous traitor in film history

**Score:** 1987
**Body:** “Ignorance is bliss.” Yeah, OK Cypher. You sell out your comrades and a cause to save the human race from extinction for a fake reality and a cushy deal.

Joe Pantoliano’s delivery was perfect for that role. The same goes for The Fugitive (1993) and Bound (1996). His performances are always balanced, not too over-the-top, and not too subtle.

“You know, I know this steak doesn’t exist. I know when I put it in my mouth, the Matrix is telling my brain that it is juicy and delicious. After nine years…you know what I realize…”

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ralph Cifareto? Was he a rat?
  - She was a hooah
    - But to me she was beautiful, Rubenesque 
  - Five fucking matrices, and we got this other pygmy thing over in Zion.
2. Ultimately, Cypher just wanted to stop living in suffering.  
Yes, he betrayed humanity and Zion. But after living in the Matrix, then seeing what reality really was and how dismal and depressing it is, I can’t blame him for wanting to reintegrate.  
Don’t agree with his decision, but I see why he made the decision.
3. Saruman is up there. 

He was meant to be a guardian of Middle Earth.
4. That slow bite into the steak with the "ignorance is bliss" line is one of the best villain moments in any 90s film. Pantoliano killed it.
5. The mark of a good bad guy - You completely understand his motivations.

Fuck eating rats underground in some dystopian hellscape. You mean the toothpaste can be put back into the tube and you can live a life of luxury in 1999?? Yes please.


He perhaps didn't have to revel in killing his crew so much though.
  - I'd fuck you all over to go back to 1999
    - Gods, I was strong then 

## 2. Harold Perrineau aged like gold

**Score:** 1546

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Best Mercutio ever. 
  - His Mercutio paired with Leguizamo's Tybalt was so impressive and dynamic that it actually made me start reading Shakespeare. 
  - He was great in Oz
2. WAAALT.
  - BUT HE'S MY *SON*!!!!!!!!!!!

It's unbearable. Like we get it, dude. I mean damn
  - WA-HA-HA-HAAALLLLT!
3. He grew a chin
  - He discovered Chin Day at the gym.
4. ![gif](giphy|jKkOOOGTHCLWo)
5. ![gif](giphy|3oAt2dA6LxMkRrGc0g)



## 3. This is extremely fucked up. Buy physical media.

**Score:** 1382

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The “play has no limits” slogan at the bottom of this email is pretty ironic. 
  - I’ve preached physical media to most all my friends for years, and it’s finally catching up to all them for loving digital media. 
    - They should have every legal right to go ahead and download those movies however they please once they purchased it. 

What bullshit. 
      - At least give refunds 😕
2. And no refund? So our purchases were just rentals all along?
  - Limited and revocable license.  No fixed duration so not really a rental.  But for practical discourse, yes.
3. This is exactly why I never stopped buying DVDs/Blu Rays
4. And that is exactly why physical media is important.
5. That sounds like play does have some limits
6. If buying isn't owning, then pirating isn't stealing

## 4. Is Scar Jo a good actress?

**Score:** 851

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She's great in Marriage Story and Jojo Rabbit
  - Lost in Translation is all I needed to see to know she’s fantastic. 
    - In "Her" she's a disembodied voice and she still gives one of the best performance ever.
2. She was stellar in Lost in translation and Jojo Rabbit. And there are a lot of smaller movies she did great.

However she did get typecasted a lot. But they mostly did good for her. But yes! Underrated in range in range i would say
3. Yes. And she’s hot and has a good sense of humor. 
  - ![gif](giphy|lUaZ1BkXPRFE4)
    - https://i.redd.it/pcxgxuozro9h1.gif
  - And a lovely singing voice.
4. She's is a really good VA as well. Her audiobook of Alice's Adventures in Wonderland is amazing. 
  - Her

## 5. Which movie villain's backstory, motivations, or personal tragedy made you empathize with them? For me, it's Zemo from Captain America: Civil War

**Score:** 681
**Body:** Daniel Brühl was excellent as the villain in Captain America: Civil War. A grounded antagonist who felt more compelling than many superpowered villains. His backstory made me empathize with him.

The dialogue below clearly showcases his motivations.

My father lived outside the city. I thought we would be safe there. My son was excited. He could see the Iron Man from the car window. I told my wife "Don't worry. They're fighting in the city. We're miles from harm". And the dust cleared... and the screaming stopped... it took me two days until I found their bodies. My father... still holding my wife and son in his arms... and the Avengers?

They went home

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|qdljIW22Re1IQ)  
Clyde Shelton in law abiding citizen. 
2. **Lt. Dixon Piper:** Why did he do it? Why did Cunth kill your wife?

**MacGruber:** To this day, I have no idea. We actually all went to college together. Believe it or not, we were very close friends. Then after graduation, he got engaged to her. He asked me to be his best man and right about that time, I started banging her and mowing her box. She was actually the first person I felt comfortable enough around to let eat out my butt. Anyway, shortly thereafter, she left him for me. She was actually carrying his child at the time. I asked her to terminate it, obviously, so we could start fresh. And she agreed. We were so in love. And he took that from me.

**Lt. Dixon Piper:** That's really fucked up.

**MacGruber:** Thanks.
  - I'm with Lt. Piper on this one
3. Christian Bale’s Gorr in Thor: Love and Thunder. It was hard not to empathize with him. The movie was slop like watching a real actor while everyone else runs around making fart jokes. You could really feel his pain come through, and it was hard to really judge him for the judgments he made and path he took.
4. ![gif](giphy|3o6Mbb8YQxwIgjBvmE)
  - This was the first movie that showed me as a kid (11) that our government did our own soldiers dirty. It was the first time I understood the grey area and got a hint of “truth”, albeit from an exaggerated Hollywood story. 

It’s also the only Michael Bay movie I genuinely love.
5. I \*loved\* him as Zemo. 
6. Zemo is amazing. Also rest in peace Chadwick 🙏
7. “The living are not done with you yet”
8. Gods the writing was good then

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. [Crosspost] Hello reddit, I'm Carla Simón, director of Alcarràs, Summer 1993, and Romería. Ask me anything!

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I organized an AMA/Q&A with Carla Simón, director of Alcarràs, Summer 1993, and Romería. All 3 of her films are critically acclaimed, with 1 premiering at Berlin and 2 at Cannes, and were all the Spain submission to the Academy Awards in their respective years. Her new film, Romeria, is out in theaters starting this week.

It's live here now in r/movies for anyone interested in asking a question:

[https://www.reddit.com/r/movies/comments/1ug6v4i/hello\_reddit\_im\_carla\_sim%C3%B3n\_director\_of\_alcarr%C3%A0s/](https://www.reddit.com/r/movies/comments/1ug6v4i/hello_reddit_im_carla_sim%C3%B3n_director_of_alcarr%C3%A0s/)

She will be back at 3 PM ET today to answer questions. I recommend asking in advance. Please ask there, not here. All questions are much appreciated!

Trailer: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=da3CAnPA\_EM](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=da3CAnPA_EM)

Synopsis: To secure her education, Marina must find her birth family. Guided by her mother's diary, she travels to the coast and uncovers buried secrets and long-hidden shames of the past.

Thank you :)

Her verification/proof photo: [https://i.imgur.com/WHI41Cp.jpeg](https://i.imgur.com/WHI41Cp.jpeg)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. Cat People (1942) - the haunting of the psyche and the femme fatale

**Score:** 19
**Body:** This is just a superlative film (maybe my 4th rewatch last night), photographically jammed with dark noir shadows, salvation halos of light, a story of a sweet, innocent (immigrant) girl holding a dangerous inner secret. Little Red Riding Hood and Wolf all in one. It has been theorized that this film is key to understanding the nature of the femme fatale in Film Noir, in some takes the natural bridge between Noir and Horror. (In this reading Film Noir films were originally female audience films, not structured so much by the male gaze...they were extensions of female audience horror films, and Cat People is seen as an early hybrid \[Dracula's Daughter in 1936 also notable\]) Femme Fatales embodied female audience fears. Some of the most heightened scenes in this film are honestly spectacular. The architect office prowl-down >!where a t-square serves a cross bathed in light!<, the street stalking scene where a bus pulls up with a cat-like hiss, out of the darkness, etc. We have the >!perverse!< psychiatrist (psychiatry a big film noir theme) and the whole wrestling with dark, subconscious drives. Some of the first femme fatales in cinema were exotic women from far away, harboring hidden powers and this film presents just such a case. Again and again she pleads "I've told you everything, and I've never lied to you!". Highly recommended influential film (the Schrader 1982 remake only if you love 80s eroto-thriller camp), that also forms an important bridge in cinema history. When she gets recognized as a "sister" by another of her people, great scene. When muddy panther footprints turn into high heel foot prints in the cement, great scene. Wonderful, memorable performance by Simone Simon.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Much agreed, it is also imo interesting to consider it from a frame of repressed sexuality as a catastrophic force, as well as the mystery and fear of the woman’s desire. This can be adduced from the representation of her ‘transformation’ into the panther(ess) once she has been moved towards deep feeling and expression. It is a feminist movie through and through, but without ever losing sight that it is a woman of the world and not above it, subject to pathological structures such as conventional gender behaviour, societal repression of sexuality, the enigma of the unconscious (her going into a non-human, unmentalisable form) — as well as the seeming sauntering ignorance of all the rest about her condition until the very bleak: “she never lied to us..”

Within the scene of the >!murder of her psychiatrist there is a very simple yet quite ample criticism of the way mental health services were rendered then, and how the different genders were thought of to present fundamental differences - e.g. ‘feminine’ hysteria - which is met with the enigmatic ‘feminine’ violence which nonetheless destroys the male doctor completely.!< Very intriguing scene, and the subtext is never forced.

There is something also intriguing about the scene wherein her ‘cat sister’ or whatnot comes in and begins her “Moi Sestra” questioning, especially with how catlike she was presented. Really good stuff, Tourner’s onyx chiaroscuro really finds capable subjects for it.

## 3. Alien: Sex and Capitalism

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I’ve been meaning to write this post after revisiting Alien as I think it was upon this viewing that I understood the depth of the monsters metaphorical implications — and why it’s so psychologically disturbing.

Firstly, I initially thought it was due to its nihilism and lack of moral framework (similar to the manner in which Spielberg depicted the shark in Jaws); however, I realise the xenomorphs truly disturbing quality is the heavily implied sexual imagery that the monstet evokes. I think this is most obvious with the facehugger — an entity that tightly wraps its limps around your face and forcefully impregnates the victim. Clearly, it’s not too fare-fetched to view this as a metaphor for sexual assault. But even more interestingly is that the first victim is male, ultimately flipping the gender tropes. As a male viewer, it’s the facehugger’s ability to completely strip the victims autonomy with its vile act that makes it so disturbing and disgusting.

Secondly, I couldn’t help but see the parallels between the monsters purity and lack of moral murkiness as a metaphor for the most purest vision of capitalism that isn’t disturbed by any moral qualms. Just like the functionality of the monster and its complete lack of any moral concerns, the way in which the corporation functions in the film also contains the exact same sense of purity that isn’t hampered by any morality or concern for human well-being. It literally sends humans into space knowing they are all most likely going to die, and that’s ok if it means that they obtain the entity. This is also a monstrous and pure form of capitalism that doesn’t concern itself with any moral framework. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's like in Aliens when Ripley says to Burke, "I don't know, you don't them fucking each other over for a promotion." I'm not sure I'd equate the alien with capitalism. There's a greed involved in capitalism that the aliens don't have. They have a survival directive, yes, but capitalism is more human. I think as a metaphor it's not 100% but I feel what you're saying. I concur about the sexual overtones of the alien and the facehugger's brutal forced entry and robbery of the victim's autonomy. For sure. Geiger always had a darkly sexual edge that made it all the more disturbing.
2. I really like you putting these two perspectives together. The xenomorph is the "perfect organism" due to its extreme adaptability (and supposedly its aggression). It's a pretty straight up mapping into how many describe Capitalism, the famous "It's easier to imagine the end of the world than the end of Capitalism" note, indicating just how completely it has its itself around our throat while it is impregnating us. And, making this a sort of "rape" (and 'unnatural" rape of men, unnatural from the hegemonic perspective) does kick in all that horror. I was also struck how when Ash attempts to kill Ripley he does it through some sort of oral penetration trope attack (I guess, suffocating her through an improvisational tube) that mirrors the face-hugger's oral air-way penetration of Kane. And...this seems to be done with a rolled up porno magazine. She literally has to breath through porn to live, just as Kane had to breathe through the Face Hugger's tube. The sexuality assault, and the relentless, "perfect" aggression of Capitalism are meant to go together and produce the horror. We are being face F-ed...so to speak, and being impregnated with the very means of our demise.

---
